variables.name: flask_principal.Need
documentation:
A required need

This is just a named tuple, and practically any tuple will do.

The `method` attribute can be used to look up element 0, and the `value`
attribute can be used to look up element 1.

variables.name: flask_principal.ItemNeed
documentation:
A required item need

An item need is just a named tuple, and practically any tuple will do. In
addition to other Needs, there is a type, for example this could be specified
as:

```
ItemNeed('update', 27, 'posts')
('update', 27, 'posts') # or like this
```

And that might describe the permission to update a particular blog post. In
reality, the developer is free to choose whatever convention the permissions
are.

classes.name: flask_principal.PermissionDenied
documentation: Permission denied to the resource

classes.name: flask_principal.Identity

methods.name: flask_principal.Identity.can
documentation:
Whether the identity has access to the permission.

```
param permission

The permission to test provision for.
```

Represent the user's identity.

```
param id

The user id

param auth_type

The authentication type used to confirm the user's
identity.
```

The identity is used to represent the user's identity in the system. This
object is created on login, or on the start of the request as loaded from
the user's session.

Once loaded it is sent using the `identity-loaded` signal, and should be
populated with additional required information.

Needs that are provided by this identity should be added to the `provides`
set after loading.

classes.name: flask_principal.AnonymousIdentity
documentation: An anonymous identity

classes.name: flask_principal.IdentityContext

methods.name: flask_principal.IdentityContext.identity
documentation: The identity of this principal

methods.name: flask_principal.IdentityContext.can
documentation: Whether the identity has access to the permission

The context of an identity for a permission.

:::{note}
The principal is usually created by the flaskext.Permission.require method
call for normal use-cases.
:::

The principal behaves as either a context manager or a decorator. The
permission is checked for provision in the identity, and if available the
flow is continued (context manager) or the function is executed (decorator).

classes.name: flask_principal.Permission

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.__init__
documentation:
A set of needs, any of which must be present in an identity to have
access.

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.__nonzero__
documentation: Equivalent to `self.can()`.

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.__bool__
documentation: Equivalent to `self.can()`.

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.__and__
documentation: Does the same thing as `self.union(other)`

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.__or__
documentation: Does the same thing as `self.difference(other)`

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.__contains__
documentation: Does the same thing as `other.issubset(self)`.

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.require
documentation:
Create a principal for this permission.

The principal may be used as a context manager, or a decroator.

If `http_exception` is passed then `abort()` will be called
with the HTTP exception code. Otherwise a `PermissionDenied`
exception will be raised if the identity does not meet the
requirements.

```
param http_exception

the HTTP exception code (403, 401 etc)
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.test
documentation:
Checks if permission available and raises relevant exception
if not. This is useful if you just want to check permission
without wrapping everything in a require() block.

This is equivalent to:

```
with permission.require():
    pass
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.reverse
documentation: Returns reverse of current state (needs->excludes, excludes->needs)

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.union
documentation:
Create a new permission with the requirements of the union of this
and other.

```
param other

The other permission
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.difference
documentation:
Create a new permission consisting of requirements in this
permission and not in the other.

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.issubset
documentation:
Whether this permission needs are a subset of another

```
param other

The other permission
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.allows

Whether the identity can access this permission.

```

The identity
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Permission.can
documentation:
Whether the required context for this permission has access

This creates an identity context and tests whether it can access this
permission

Represents needs, any of which must be present to access a resource

```
param needs

The needs for this permission
```

classes.name: flask_principal.Denial

classes.name: flask_principal.Principal

methods.name: flask_principal.Principal.set_identity
documentation:
Set the current identity.

```
param identity

The identity to set
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Principal.identity_loader
documentation:
Decorator to define a function as an identity loader.

An identity loader function is called before request to find any
provided identities. The first found identity is used to load from.

For example:

```
app = Flask(__name__)

principals = Principal(app)

@principals.identity_loader
def load_identity_from_weird_usecase():
    return Identity('ali')
```

methods.name: flask_principal.Principal.identity_saver
documentation:
Decorator to define a function as an identity saver.

An identity loader saver is called when the identity is set to persist
it for the next request.

For example:

```
app = Flask(__name__)

principals = Principal(app)

@principals.identity_saver
def save_identity_to_weird_usecase(identity):
    my_special_cookie['identity'] = identity
```

Principal extension

```
param app

The flask application to extend

param use_sessions

Whether to use sessions to extract and store
identification.

param skip_static

Whether to ignore static endpoints.
```
